region_of_origin: Middle East / Balkans (served in Singapore context)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for burek; closest defensible match is meat-filled pastry food product (FOODON_03315091).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP (Meat pies and pasties) is the closest defensible category for a baked minced-beef filo pastry product. No exact burek code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for burek or equivalent meat-filled pastry dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of beef burek is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one medium-sized pastry roll as typically served in a Singaporean Middle Eastern eatery.

nutrient_source.title: Burek Beef — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Burek Beef (cr_id G00010156). Saturated fat not reported in HPB record; estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for beef-filled pastry (USDA SR Legacy #21065 and #18024 composite) and documented below.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on meat-filled pastry / börek category
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef burek. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on the closest analogous food: meat-filled filo/börek pastry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database, which report GI values of 40–50 for similar meat-and-pastry products with low net carbohydrate content. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (45 × 6.29) / 100 ≈ 2.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 369 mg per 100 g; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 554 mg sodium, representing ~25% of the 2,000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit beef burek to one small serving (≤100 g) and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods such as pickles, soy sauce, or processed condiments to keep daily sodium within target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low net carbohydrate (4.19 g/100 g), low sugar (1.45 g/100 g), and high protein (18.48 g/100 g) support glycaemic control; estimated GI of 45 places this in the low-GI category.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beef burek is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate single-serving portions; the high protein content slows gastric emptying and blunts postprandial glucose rise, but portion control is essential to avoid cumulative refined carbohydrate load from filo pastry.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2.3 g/100 g), cholesterol (52 mg/100 g), sodium (369 mg/100 g), and trace trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) from filo pastry preparation collectively elevate cardiovascular risk markers.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should consume beef burek occasionally and in small portions (≤100 g), request preparation with minimal added fat, and ensure the overall dietary pattern is low in saturated fat and sodium to offset the cumulative lipid and blood-pressure burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Minced beef is a primary dietary source of arachidonic acid, which drives TNF-alpha and IL-6 pro-inflammatory pathways; trace trans fat from filo pastry further amplifies joint inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise red-meat pastry products; consider chicken or spinach burek as lower-inflammatory alternatives, and confirm that butter or margarine is not used in pastry preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef burek (also written beef börek or burek daging) is a baked savoury pastry made from thin, layered filo dough wrapped around a seasoned minced-beef filling. The dish originates from the Balkan and Middle Eastern culinary tradition and is widely available in Singapore at Middle Eastern and halal eateries. The pastry is rolled or coiled, then baked until the exterior is golden and crispy while the interior remains moist and flavourful. Per 100 g, beef burek provides 148 kcal, 18.5 g protein, 6.0 g fat, and only 4.2 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate savoury snack relative to its caloric density. Sodium is elevated at 369 mg/100 g due to salt in both the filling and the dough. The estimated glycaemic index is 45 (low), and a standard 150 g serving delivers a glycaemic load of approximately 2.8. Clinically, the low carbohydrate and high protein profile is favourable for glycaemic control in Type 2 Diabetes, but the sodium content warrants caution for hypertensive individuals, and the saturated fat and cholesterol content are relevant for cardiovascular disease management. The dish is generally considered halal when prepared with certified beef and oil-based pastry.
common_user_queries: How many calories in beef burek?
Can I eat burek if I have high blood pressure?
How much protein is in beef burek?
What is the GI of beef burek?
Is burek good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010156, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite for beef-filled pastry and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for meat-filled filo pastry, clearly flagged as estimated.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.3 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (2.5 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.6 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for lean ground beef (#21065) and filo pastry (#18024) in approximate proportions consistent with the HPB total fat value of 6.0 g/100 g; these are not directly reported in the HPB record. 2. Trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) is an estimate based on typical filo pastry preparation using margarine or shortening; this value is flagged as approximate. 3. Fields set to -1 (vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g): these micronutrients and water content are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish, and no sufficiently close composite entry in USDA FoodData Central provides all of these values for a beef-filo pastry product with confidence; they are therefore set to -1 per the numeric hygiene rule. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for beef burek was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. 5. The input record listed primary_cuisine as 'Middle Eastern'; this is retained. Burek has Balkan origins but is widely prepared across the Middle East and is sold in Singapore as a Middle Eastern dish. 6. Halal flag set to true based on the dish description (minced beef, filo pastry, no pork or alcohol ingredients noted); this should be confirmed with the specific vendor.
